mission.title: Install and Configure Kgateway on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kgateway is a cloud-native API gateway designed for microservices, providing a resilient and performance-oriented control plane that implements the Kubernetes Gateway API. Installing kgateway allows you to manage API connectivity and apply policies like authentication and rate limiting seamlessly across your services.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the kgateway Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kgateway https://kgateway.dev/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the CRDs
steps.description:
Install the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required by kgateway using Helm:
```bash
helm install kgateway-crds kgateway/kgateway-crds --namespace kgateway --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Install kgateway
steps.description:
Deploy the kgateway API gateway using Helm with the following command:
```bash
helm install kgateway kgateway/kgateway --namespace kgateway --create-namespace --version v2.2.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the kgateway pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kgateway
```

steps.title: Configure a BackendConfigPolicy
steps.description:
Create a BackendConfigPolicy resource to apply circuit breaker limits to a service. Save the following configuration to a file named `backendconfigpolicy.yaml`:
```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.kgateway.dev/v1alpha1
kind: BackendConfigPolicy
metadata:
  name: httpbin-cb
  namespace: default
spec:
  targetRefs:
  - group: ""
    kind: Service
    name: httpbin
  circuitBreakers:
    maxConnections: 1000
    maxPendingRequests: 500
    maxRequests: 2000
    maxRetries: 10
```
Then apply it:
```bash
kubectl apply -f backendconfigpolicy.yaml
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the kgateway pods running in the kgateway namespace. You can verify this by running `kubectl get pods --namespace kgateway` and seeing the pods in a 'Running' state. Additionally, you will have configured a BackendConfigPolicy for your services.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kgateway https://kgateway.dev/charts
helm repo update
helm install kgateway kgateway/kgateway --namespace kgateway --create-namespace --version v2.2.1

apiVersion: gateway.kgateway.dev/v1alpha1
kind: BackendConfigPolicy
metadata:
  name: httpbin-cb
  namespace: default
spec:
  targetRefs:
  - group: ""
    kind: Service
    name: httpbin
  circuitBreakers:
    maxConnections: 1000
    maxPendingRequests: 500
    maxRequests: 2000
    maxRetries: 10

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the kgateway release from the kgateway namespace using Helm. This command will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall kgateway --namespace kgateway
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent volumes associated with the kgateway installation. This ensures that no leftover resources remain in the cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall kgateway-crds --namespace kgateway
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace kgateway
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the kgateway namespace to clean up any remaining resources and verify that it has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kgateway
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of the kgateway installation. You can export the current resources to YAML files for safekeeping.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kgateway -o yaml > kgateway-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the kgateway installation to the latest version. Ensure you specify the desired version if not upgrading to the latest.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kgateway kgateway/kgateway --namespace kgateway --version v2.3.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check that the kgateway pods are running the new version and that there are no issues.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kgateway
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> --namespace kgateway
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If kgateway pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that may indicate the cause of the crash.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kgateway
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If services are not reachable through the kgateway, verify that the BackendConfigPolicy is correctly configured and applied. Check the status of the policy.
```bash
kubectl get backendconfigpolicy --namespace default
kubectl describe backendconfigpolicy httpbin-cb --namespace default
```

troubleshooting.title: Ingress rules not applied
troubleshooting.description:
If ingress rules are not being applied, ensure that the kgateway is correctly configured to handle ingress traffic. Check the kgateway configuration.
```bash
kubectl get gateways --namespace kgateway
kubectl describe gateway <gateway-name> --namespace kgateway
```

troubleshooting.title: High latency in API responses
troubleshooting.description:
If you are experiencing high latency in API responses, check the resource limits and requests for the kgateway pods. Adjust them if necessary.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kgateway -o=jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].resources}'
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
